text: Vertical stiffness is moderately negatively correlated with running economy (lower oxygen cost), meaning stiffer vertical spring mechanics are associated with better running economy.
Leg stiffness is moderately negatively correlated with running economy, indicating that greater leg stiffness is associated with lower energy cost during running.
Knee stiffness has a small negative correlation with running economy, suggesting higher knee stiffness is associated with modestly better economy.
The benefit of leg stiffness for running economy is greater at higher speeds, with speed explaining 79% of the variance in the leg stiffness–economy relationship.
Runners with higher VO2max benefit more from leg stiffness for running economy, with VO2max explaining 92% of the variance in the leg stiffness–economy relationship.
Higher aerobic fitness and faster running speeds are prerequisites for runners to fully exploit leg stiffness to minimize energy expenditure.
